name: Calvert Hill Mine
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding site in southwestern Montana offering quartz and agate collecting at 7195 ft elevation. Personal use collecting allowed with minimal restrictions.
description:
Calvert Hill Mine sits on BLM-managed land in Beaverhead County, Montana, at 7195 feet elevation in the high valley terrain of southwestern Montana. The site is accessible to rockhounders seeking quartz and chalcedony specimens typical of the region's varied mineral deposits. The flat terrain makes this location approachable for collectors of varied experience levels, though the high elevation and seasonal snowfall affect access windows.

The geology of this region reflects the Basin and Range extension that shaped southwestern Montana during the Tertiary period. Silica-rich fluids deposited along fracture zones created the quartz veins and secondary chalcedony that collectors find here today. The Beaverhead area has a long history of small-scale prospecting and mining dating back to the 1800s, with many claims worked intermittently for various minerals.

Collecting at Calvert Hill Mine is strictly for personal use under federal regulations. The BLM allows recreational rockhounding without a permit on this public land, but you are limited to 25 pounds per day per the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). This is a self-service collecting area with no developed facilities, so come prepared with water and basic tools. The surrounding landscape is high-elevation semi-arid with sparse vegetation, offering views across the Beaverhead valley.

Best visits occur from late spring through early fall when the roads are passable and weather permits safe fieldwork. Winter snowfall can close access, and mud and standing water are common in spring. Bring sturdy boots, digging tools, buckets, and plenty of water. The flat terrain minimizes hiking but does not eliminate the need for careful footing over rocky ground.
history: Calvert Hill Mine sits within the Beaverhead metallogenic belt of southwestern Montana, where Tertiary-age volcanic and hydrothermal activity created widespread silica mineralization. Quartz veins and chalcedony deposits in this region formed along fracture zones during regional extension, making the Beaverhead area historically significant for small-scale hardrock prospecting since the late 1800s.
tips: Search the exposed hillsides and small wash areas where weathering has exposed quartz and chalcedony nodules in the decomposed host rock. Bring a small pickaxe or crack hammer to work specimens free from weathered host material; simple prying often extracts pieces without heavy excavation. Look for white to milky quartz and banded chalcedony with warm colors - specimens here tend to be modest in size but often display good clarity.
terrain_notes: Elevation 7195 ft in a relatively flat high-valley setting. Sparse sagebrush and grasses dominate; scattered rocks and small outcrops dot the landscape. Footing is uneven with loose volcanic rocks and decomposed granite scattered across the ground. Walking distance from parking is typically short (less than 0.5 miles) but footing requires sturdy boots.
best_season: June - September. High elevation means snow blocks access from November through May. Summer weather is mild at this altitude; afternoon thunderstorms common July - August.
written_directions: From Dillon, take MT Highway 278 east for approximately 22 miles toward Wisdom. Turn south on the signed BLM access road near Calvert. Follow the dirt road for approximately 3 miles to the Calvert Hill Mine area. Parking is available at a small pullout near the old mine workings. High-clearance vehicle recommended, especially after rain or snowmelt.
road_conditions: Dirt road suitable for high-clearance vehicles; can become muddy or rutted after rain or snowmelt. Washboard surface common. No winter maintenance.
parking_notes: Small dirt pullout near old mine workings. Space for 3-4 vehicles. No developed facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Pack out all trash. Do not dig into bedrock or create new excavations; collect from surface or previously disturbed areas only.

nearest_services: Dillon, Montana (approximately 25 miles west) has gas, food, lodging, and supplies. Wisdom, Montana (approximately 12 miles east) has limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with uneven rocky footing. Loose volcanic rocks require careful stepping. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots essential. Short walks from parking, but high elevation (7195 ft) may cause mild altitude effects for lowland visitors.

faq.question: What gems can I find at Calvert Hill Mine in Montana?
faq.answer: Calvert Hill Mine is known for quartz and chalcedony specimens. The site yields white to milky quartz and banded chalcedony in modest sizes. Specimens often display good clarity and interesting color banding typical of high-elevation Montana deposits.

faq.question: Is a permit required for rockhounding at Calvert Hill Mine?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Calvert Hill Mine sits on BLM public land in Montana where recreational rockhounding is allowed for personal use. You are limited to 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations, and commercial collection is strictly prohibited.

faq.question: What is the best season to visit Calvert Hill Mine?
faq.answer: The best season is June through September. The site sits at 7195 feet elevation, so heavy snow blocks access from November through May. Summer weather is mild, though afternoon thunderstorms are common in July and August.

faq.question: Can I bring my family and kids rockhounding at Calvert Hill Mine?
faq.answer: Yes, Calvert Hill Mine is family-friendly for children ages 6 and up. The flat terrain and short walking distances make it accessible for families. The site has no developed facilities, so bring water and snacks. Supervise children around loose rocks and uneven footing.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring to Calvert Hill Mine rockhounding?
faq.answer: Bring a small pickaxe or crack hammer, digging bar, sturdy boots, buckets, and plenty of water. The high elevation and remote location mean you should also pack a first aid kit, extra clothing, and a map. Specimens here are typically worked free from host rock with simple hand tools rather than heavy excavation.

faq.question: How far is Calvert Hill Mine from Dillon, Montana and what are the driving directions?
faq.answer: Calvert Hill Mine is approximately 25 miles from Dillon. Take MT Highway 278 east for about 22 miles toward Wisdom, then turn south on the signed BLM access road near Calvert. Follow the dirt road about 3 miles to the parking area. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended, especially after rain.
